text: The video begins at the thoracic portion of the Ivor Lewis esophagectomy.
A thoracoscope has been inserted through a planned chest tube incision for video recording.
The patient's spine is towards screen left and the lung is retracted anteriorly towards screen right.
The gastric conduit is visualized in the foreground with a lesser curve gastric conduit staple line facing laterally.
A uniform diameter conduit is created in the abdomen with an initial fire of a 100 millimeter stapler using a green cartridge which delivers two rows of staples with a 4.8 millimeter staple height.
Completion of the conduit construction usually requires one or two additional fires of a 60 millimeter endoscopic tri stapler.
Tri staplers are not used on the conduit tip.
The gastric conduit has been delivered into the chest.
The esophagus has been mobilized only to the level of the conduit tip to minimize devascularization.

To begin the esophagogastric anastomosis, the mid-left lateral aspect of the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus is aligned over the lesser curve staple line with four initial tacking sutures placed approximately three centimeters apart.
A shorter side-to-side anastomosis can be performed if needed to achieve an adequate proximal esophageal margin.
The esophagus is partially transected one centimeter distal to the inferior set of tacking sutures.

In cases of malignancy where the surgical margin may be close, the esophagus can be completely transected and a ring of esophagus from the surgical specimen is sent for frozen section pathologic examination prior to proceeding.
A corresponding two centimeter gastrotomy is made in the conduit across the lesser curve staple line.
A total of five tacking sutures are placed between the adjacent conduit and esophageal walls.
The first is placed in the middle through the lesser curve staple line.
Two sutures are placed on either side.
The stapled portion of the anastomosis, which represents about two-thirds of the total anastomotic diameter, is then performed with an endoscopic stapler.
Seen here is a linear 45 millimeter green cartridge with a 4.1 millimeter staple height.
The narrow anvil is placed within the esophageal lumen and the large reload jaw placed within the conduit lumen.
After a pause, the stapler is fired, restapling and cutting through the previous lesser curve staple line.
The side-to-side communication is inspected to assure complete cutting and stapling has occurred.
The esophageal transection is then completed sharply.
A silk suture is then placed at the center of the esophageal opening for retraction.
The length of the cut end of the esophagus is typically longer than the gastrotomy.
As such, an ellipse of conduit is removed over the lesser curve staple line rather than extending the gastrotomy laterally in order to preserve conduit collateral blood supply.
The open common lumen is closed in two layers of sutures, beginning with an inner layer of inverted-interrupted 3O polyglactin suture, which are tied after all sutures have been placed.
A second layer of interrupted 3-0 silk suture using a limbered technique are placed beginning at both corners.
A modified horizontal mattress suture is placed at the junction of the gastric conduit staple line.
The initial orienting sutures are cut and the completed anastomosis is inspected.

Soft tissue coverage is usually accomplished with a pleural flap mobilized from either superiorly over the upper third of the intrathoracic esophagus, as seen here, or lateral body wall downward and secured to the conduit with interrupted 3-0 silk sutures.
In heavily radiated tissues, consideration can be given to bulkier coverage, such as with pericardial fat.
Typically, between suture closure of the open common lumen and tacking the pleura to the conduit, any residual conduit redundancy is eliminated.
The superior aspect of the lesser curve conduit staple line can be over-sewn using a limbered technique extending inferiorly until the right gastric vessels are encountered.
A nasogastric tube is passed by anesthesia into the conduit to the level of the cruce prior to chest closure.
Chest tubes are placed and kept to water seal.
Esophagectomy plays an important role in the treatment of esophageal cancer and select benign conditions.
Gastroesophageal and asthmatic complications, such as leak and stricture, are associated with morbidity, occasional mortality, and increased cost of care.
We believe this novel side-to-side, staple line on staple line technique is easily adapted and provides excellent results with respect to minimizing and asthmatic complications.
